text: The surgeon uses robotic instruments to mobilize the liver by separating it from its attachments, carefully dissecting through areas of scar tissue. The robotic tools provide enhanced articulation, allowing precise movements to detach the liver from the posterior structures.

The surgeon uses an intraoperative ultrasound device to visualize the tumor within the liver, scanning through the liver tissue to ensure no other lesions are present. The ultrasound image is displayed on the screen for precise examination.

The surgeon uses an ultrasound to identify the tumor area in the liver, then scores and coagulates a line on the liver surface with a harmonic scalpel. This marks the planned resection area, ensuring a margin of healthy liver tissue is maintained around the tumor.

The surgeon controls robotic instruments to perform coagulation through the liver, ensuring a hemostatic approach to minimize blood loss. The assistant uses a suction aspirator to manage fluids, while gentle retraction is applied to the liver. Instruments are prepared to grasp vessels in case of bleeding.

The surgeon rotates the liver upward to access and dissect along its posterior border. Metal clips are visible on the structures where the gallbladder was previously attached, indicating its removal to clear the resection margin. The dissection continues through the liver to facilitate specimen removal.

The surgeon uses coagulation to cauterize the resection margin of the liver, preventing any oozing from the cut surface. The entire liver surface is covered, and a hemostatic agent is occasionally applied to ensure hemostasis.
